Prime numbers are positive integers greater than 1 with exactly two factors, 1 and the number itself. Examples include 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13. A non-prime integer is called composite.

1 is neither prime nor composite.

Twin Prime Conjecture: There are infinitely many prime numbers \(p\) for which is \(p+2\) also a prime number. Examples include 3 (as 5 is prime), 17 (as 19 is prime) and 41 (as 43 is prime).

Goldbach's conjecture: Every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers.

A Mersenne prime is a prime number in the form \(2^p - 1\) for some prime \(p\).

Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: Every positive integer can be written as a product of primes (possibly with repetition).

If \(n \gt 1\), then \(n\) is said to be powerful if all exponents in it's prime factorization are at least 2.
Which of the following integers are prime: 127, 129, 131, 133, 137, 139.
If \(p\) is prime and \(p|ab\), then prove that either \(p|a\) or \(p|b\).
Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers.
Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers of the form \(4k+3\).
Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers of the form \(3k+2\).
Verify Goldbach's conjecture for the following integers: 30, 98 and 222
Prove or disprove that no prime number can be expressed as a4-b4
Prove or disprove that for any positive integer \(n\), there are at least \(n\) consecutive composite integers
Prove or disprove that if \(p\) is prime, then 2p - 1 is prime.
Let p be 11, then 211 - 1 is 2047, which is 23 X 89.
Prove that if \(2^p - 1\) is prime, then \(p\) is prime.
Prove that if \(n \gt 1\) and \(a^n - 1\) is prime, then \(a=2\).
Prove that if \(a^n + 1\) is an odd prime, then \(a\) is even and \(n\) is a power of 2.
Prove that if n2+1 is prime, then n2 = 4k
Prove that a powerful number is a product of a square and a cube.
Prove that any integer greater than 6 can be represented as a sum of two relatively prime integers.
For n ≥ 1, prove or disprove that there are infinite prime of the form n3 + 1.

We can rewrite (n3 + 1) as (n+1)(n2-n+1).

If n ≥ 1, then both factors would be more than 1.
Prove or disprove that if the smallest prime \(p\) of the positive integer \(n\) exceeds \(\sqrt[3]{n}\), then \(n/p\) must be prime or 1.
Show that there are no prime \(p\), such that \(p+2\) and \(p+4\) are also primes, with the exception of \(p=3\).

All positive integers (including primes) are of the form \(3k\), \(3k+1\) or \(3k+2\), where \(k \ge 0\).

If \(p=3k\), then the only time \(p\) is prime is when \(k=1\), but we ar looking for cases other than \(p=3\).

If \(p=3k+1\), then \(p+2=3k+3\), which is divisible by 3, so it's never a prime.

If \(p=3k+2\), then \(p+4=3k+6\), which is also divisible by 3.

Therefore, the integers \(p\), \(p+2\) and \(p+4\) can never together be prime, except when \(p=3\).
Show that every integer greater than 11 is the sum of two composite integers.
Let \(n\) be a positive integer greater than 3 and let \(p\) be a prime such that \(2n/3 \lt p \le n\). Show that \(p\) does not divide the binomial coefficient \({2n \choose n}\).

Use the fundamental theorem of arithmetic to prove that the root of x<sup>n</sup>+c<sub>n-1</sub>x<sup>n-1</sup>+...+c<sub>1</sub>x+c<sub>0</sub> is either an integer or an irrational, where \(c_0,c_1, \ldots, c_{n-1}\) are integers.
Prove that \(\log_p(b)\) is irrational, where \(p\) is prime and \(b\) is a positive integer that is not a power of \(p\).
